data.title: Trump Administration Sues California Over Vehicle Emissions Rules -- OPIS
data.description:
The Trump administration filed a lawsuit against California on Thursday, challenging vehicle emissions regulations that officials say amount to an unlawful electric vehicle mandate.

The suit brought by the Transportation Department targets California's Advanced Clean Cars II rules, which aim to phase out sales of new gasoline-powered vehicles, require automakers to sell increasing numbers of zero-emission cars and set stricter tailpipe emissions limits.

The administration argues the regulations violate the Energy Policy and Conservation Act of 1975, which directs the federal government to establish uniform fuel economy standards nationwide. Federal officials say California's requirements would force automakers to make rapid changes to vehicle production and increase the price of new cars.

"Oppressive, expensive electric vehicle mandates drive up costs for American consumers and violate federal law," said Attorney General Pamela Bondi.

National Highway Traffic Safety Administration Administrator Jonathan Morrison said the litigation would allow automakers to produce vehicles under a national framework rather than navigating a "costly patchwork quilt of individual state fuel economy requirements."

California has continued enforcing stricter vehicle standards despite congressional action last year rescinding the state's authority to ban sales of new gas-powered cars after 2035. The state received federal approval in 2022 for earlier vehicle standards known as Advanced Clean Cars I, which remain in effect.

California officials say the rules reduce air pollution and save drivers money on fuel over time despite higher upfront vehicle costs.

The lawsuit is the latest in a series of legal challenges by the Trump administration targeting California's vehicle emissions authority. In August, the administration filed a suit challenging the state's emissions standards for heavy-duty trucks.

The case comes amid broader federal efforts to roll back vehicle emissions limits and reduce incentives for electric vehicle adoption. Last year, President Trump signed legislation eliminating a federal tax credit for electric vehicle purchases, and last month, the Environmental Protection Agency finalized a rule that repealed federal greenhouse gas emissions standards for vehicles.


This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.


--Reporting by Allegra Fradkin, afradkin@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Colgate-Palmolive Company Announces Board Changes
data.description:
On March 12, 2026, the Board of Directors of Colgate-Palmolive Company (Colgate-Palmolive) elected Christopher S. Boerner, Ph.D., Board Chair and Chief Executive Officer of Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (Bristol Myers Squibb), to the Colgate-Palmolive Board, effective March 15, 2026. Dr. Boerner, 55, has served as Chief Executive Officer of Bristol Myers Squibb, a global biopharmaceutical company, since November 2023 and as Board Chair since April 2024 and will bring extensive global business leadership experience and expertise in the pharmaceutical and healthcare industries, complementary industries, to Colgate-Palmolive?s Board. He previously served as Executive Vice President, Chief Operating Officer of Bristol Myers Squibb in 2023 and as Executive Vice President, Chief Commercialization Officer from 2018 to 2023.

Prior to those roles, he served as Head of International Markets and Head of U.S. Commercial Markets after joining Bristol Myers Squibb in 2015. Prior to joining Bristol Myers Squibb, he held leadership roles of increasing responsibility at Seattle Genetics Inc. from 2010 to 2015 and served in marketing leadership roles at Genentech from 2002 to 2010. Earlier in his career, Dr. Boerner worked for McKinsey & Company serving global pharmaceutical and biotechnology clients.

Dr. Boerner will receive compensation as a non-employee director in accordance with Colgate-Palmolive?s director compensation program described in Colgate-Palmolive?s proxy statement filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission on March 26, 2025. The Company also announced that Steven A. Cahillane advised the Board that he will not stand for reelection to the Board at the Annual Meeting of Stockholders to be held on May 8, 2026.

data.title: Colgate-Palmolive Company Announces Quarterly Cash Dividend, Payable on May 15, 2026
data.description:
The Board of Directors of Colgate-Palmolive Company announced an increase in the quarterly common stock cash dividend to $0.53 up from $0.52 per share. The increase will be effective in the second quarter, 2026. The Board declared that the second quarter dividend is to be paid on May 15, 2026, to shareholders of record on April 20, 2026.

On an annualized basis, the new dividend rate is $2.12 versus $2.08 per share previously.

Utilities Shares Rose as Investors Seek Safety -- Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers rose as investors rotated into perceived safe haven sectors.

U.S. natural gas inventories declined by a smaller-than-usual 38 billion cubic feet last week, reducing the storage deficit against the five-year average to 17 Bcf from 43 Bcf the week before, the EIA reported.

Germany's RWE plans to invest around $20 billion in renewable energy and gas-fired power plants in the U.S. as it seeks to cash in on growing energy demand from data centers.

data.title: Technology Shares Fell Amid Broad Selloff Fueled by Inflation Fears -- Technology Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies fell as inflation fears dimmed the outlook for interest rate cuts.

The capital-intensive sector tends to benefit from falling borrowing costs that spur technology investment. But the resumption of rate cuts by the Fed looks increasingly unlikely this year.

Oracle will spend $500 million more on restructuring costs in the current fiscal year than previously reported as artificial-intelligence models allow the company to shrink parts of its workforce.

Atlassian is cutting about 10% of its workforce in a move to adapt to the rise of artificial intelligence.


Write to Paulo Trevisani at paulo.trevisani@wsj.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Financial Shares Fell as Interest Rate Cuts Look Less Likely -- Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions fell as the war in Iran makes the outlook for global interest rates less certain.

Odds of no interest rate cuts by the Fed this year rose to 45% on the CME's FedWatch tool, from 17% a week ago and just 5% a month ago, reflecting market concerns that uncertainty could hold the central bank's hand for longer than previously expected.

Deutsche Bank said it had a $30 billion private-credit exposure for the year of 2025. This marks an increase of 6% on the previous year's exposure.

Materials Shares Gave Back Early Gains -- Materials Roundup
data.description:
Shares in major agricultural commodity producers closed lower, reversing an early increase stemming from fears of global fertilizer shortages caused by the continuing blockage of the Strait of Hormuz.

Chemical companies with production away from the war zone are expected to benefit as the conflict in the Mideast hampers local activity. Analysts at Citi advised buying stock in Dow and LyondellBasell Industries on Thursday. Both were among the day's top gainers.

Algoma Steel widened its loss in the fourth quarter and posted lower revenue as the company contends with U.S. steel tariffs that continue to weigh on results.

Energy Shares Rose Amid Fears of Lasting Oil Supply Disruption -- Energy Roundup
data.description:
Energy stocks rose as crude prices kept rising and the war in Iran gave no sign of relief.

President Trump said stopping the Iranian regime from getting nuclear weapons is a higher priority than oil prices, while the EIA slashed its forecast for oil-supply growth.

Iran's new leader says his country will fight on and keep the Strait of Hormuz closed.

Occidental Petroleum and Phillips 66 were among the day's top gainers.

Stryker Says Cyberattack Disruption Is Continuing
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton


Stryker said a cyberattack related to the Iranian conflict is still disrupting its operations, including order processing, manufacturing and shipping.

Stryker experienced a global disruption to its Microsoft systems following a cyberattack Wednesday, which resulted in the company asking 56,000 employees to disconnect from all networks and avoid turning on company devices.

The hackers behind the attack said they were retaliating on behalf of Iran, The Wall Street Journal reported Wednesday.

On Thursday, Stryker said operations were still disrupted, but it doesn't believe its patient-related services or connected products have been impacted.

Stryker's investigation into the incident is ongoing, it said.


Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-12-26 1742ET

data.title: North Korea says Japan's missile expansion is raising regional security risks
data.description:
SEOUL, March 13 (Reuters) - North Korea accused Japan of heightening regional security risks by accelerating the deployment and development of long-range missiles, saying Tokyo's military buildup amounted to preparations for a future attack, state media KCNA said on Friday.

KCNA said in a commentary that Japan had begun deploying an upgraded version of its Type 12 surface-to-ship missile in Kumamoto and planned to position other long-range systems across multiple prefectures, putting neighbouring countries within range.

The commentary said Japan was pouring resources into extending missile ranges and adapting them for launches from land, sea and air, calling the systems "clearly long-range offensive weapons."

KCNA dismissed Japan's claim that the buildup was for self-defence, saying it reflected ambitions to secure attack capabilities and was part of "war preparations for re-invasion."

It added Japan's expanded purchases of long-range strike weapons and participation in joint military drills showed the country was moving closer to using those capabilities and warned Tokyo risked serious consequences if it continued on its current path.

(Reporting by Kyu-seok Shim; Editing by Chris Reese and Jamie Freed)
